anchor.snippet: a rotation is a rigid body movement which, unlike a translation
All rigid body movements are rotations, translations, or combinations of the two.
the rotations around a point/axis form a group
Rotations around the x , y and z axes are called principal rotations
any spatial rotation can be decomposed into a combination of principal rotations

annotations.label: Composition of 3D rotations about a fixed point

anchor.snippet: The combination of any sequence of rotations of an object in three dimensions about a fixed point is always equivalent to a rotation about an axis
2-dimensional rotations, unlike the 3-dimensional ones, possess no axis of rotation

annotations.label: No real eigenvector of a 2D rotation

anchor.snippet: no real vector in the plane is kept unchanged by A

anchor.section: Rotation angle and axis in 3 dimensions
anchor.snippet: Knowing that the trace is an invariant

anchor.section: Rotation angle and axis in 3 dimensions
anchor.snippet: in 3D space has an axis of rotation

anchor.section: Rotation angle and axis in 3 dimensions
anchor.snippet: any improper orthogonal 3x3 matrix may be decomposed as a proper rotation
every tridimensional rotation has a plane, which is perpendicular to the rotation axis
A vector is said to be rotating if it changes its orientation
a rotating vector always has a non-zero perpendicular component of its rate of change vector against the vector itself
The ratio of torque τ to the angular acceleration α is given by the moment of inertia
any change in orientation can be described by rotation about an axis through a chosen reference point
any motion of rigid bodies can be treated as a composition of rotation and translation
Euler rotations provide an alternative description of a rotation
A system which behaves the same regardless of how it is oriented in space is said to be rotationally invariant
if the action (the integral over time of its Lagrangian) of a physical system is invariant under rotation, then angular momentum is conserved
